title: Introduce configuration hook for UndonatableCommonPjRtBuffer in CommonPjRtClient
body:
Introduce configuration hook for UndonatableCommonPjRtBuffer in CommonPjRtClient

Introduces a new virtual method `use_undonatable_buffer()` to `CommonPjRtClient`. When an overriding client opts in by returning true, buffers will be instantiated as `UndonatableCommonPjRtBuffer` instead of the default `CommonPjRtBufferImpl`.

These undonatable buffers rely on external reference counting for their lifetimes (without internal hold tracking) and cannot be donated. This allows specific hardware backends to optimize and control buffer lifecycle management directly without framework interference.

title: PR #45821: [HLO] Validate the dimensions attribute in the HLO parser to avoid crashes
body:
PR #45821: [HLO] Validate the dimensions attribute in the HLO parser to avoid crashes

Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45821

### Problem
Several opcode handlers extract or use a value from the `dimensions={...}` attribute without validating it, so malformed-but-lexable HLO text crashes the parser (a `std::out_of_range` throw, an out-of-bounds read, or a `CHECK`-fail) instead of returning an error `Status`.

**Empty `dimensions={}`** — `all-gather`, `all-gather-start`, `reduce-scatter`, and `get-dimension-size` / `set-dimension-size` extract `dimensions->at(0)`, but the parser only checks that the required attribute is *present*, not non-empty. An empty list therefore throws `std::out_of_range`:
```
all-gather(input), replica_groups={}, dimensions={}
reduce-scatter(input), replica_groups={}, to_apply=add, dimensions={}
get-dimension-size(p), dimensions={}
```

**Out-of-range dimension value** — `scan` checks `dimensions->size() == 1` but then uses the value as `operands[0]->shape().dimensions(scan_dim)` with no range check, so an out-of-range value reads out of bounds (and the downstream `ShapeUtil::InsertDimensionAtIndex` `CHECK`-fails):
```
scan(input, init), dimensions={-1}, num_carries=0, to_apply=add
```

### Fix
- Require `dimensions->size() == 1` in the all-gather / reduce-scatter / get-dimension-size / set-dimension-size handlers before extracting `dimensions->at(0)`, matching the checks the sibling `kSort` and `kAllToAll` handlers already have. (`all-reduce`/`all-reduce-start` share the reduce-scatter parse block and don't take a `dimensions` attribute, so the new check is guarded by `opcode == kReduceScatter`.)
- Range-check `scan`'s dimension against the operand rank and return a parse error if it is out of range.

### Tests
Four `HloParserTest` cases assert that `all-gather`, `reduce-scatter`, and `get-dimension-size` with `dimensions={}`, and `scan` with an out-of-range dimension, now return an error instead of crashing.

---
Disclosure: this contribution was authored with an AI coding assistant (Claude) and reviewed before submission.

Copybara import of the project:

--

it, crashing the parser on malformed-but-lexable text instead of returning an
error Status:

- all-gather/all-gather-start, reduce-scatter and get/set-dimension-size extract
  dimensions->at(0) but only check that the required attribute is present, so
  `dimensions={}` throws std::out_of_range. Require size()==1 (matching kSort and
  kAllToAll); the reduce-scatter check is guarded by opcode since it shares the
  all-reduce block.
- scan checks size()==1 but uses the value as operands[0]->shape().dimensions(

title: Fix iterator invalidation in LegalizeSchedulingAnnotations
body:
Fix iterator invalidation in LegalizeSchedulingAnnotations

Use absl::erase_if when removing trivial scheduling groups to prevent map
mutation during range-based for loop iteration. Previously, calling
map.erase() inside a range-for loop over comp_inst_vector and
annotation_to_instruction invalidated iterators and caused assertion
failures when an annotation group spanned multiple computations.

title: [XLA:GPU] Enable AnnotationStack earlier in CuptiTracer and capture annotations for memset events.
body:
[XLA:GPU] Enable AnnotationStack earlier in CuptiTracer and capture annotations for memset events.

Otherwise the line may have the events without annotation_id. As a result of that the entire line will be marked as CUPTI Overhead.

The AnnotationStack is now enabled before API tracing begins, ensuring all API calls are potentially annotated. The AnnotationStack is also disabled if API or activity tracing fails to initialize. Memset events now include associated annotation, NVTX range, and scope range ID looked up from the AnnotationMap.

title: PR #45560: Gate unsound inverse-function folds behind fast-math
body:
PR #45560: Gate unsound inverse-function folds behind fast-math

Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45560

`ln(exp(A)) => A` and `sqrt(A*A) => |A|` in `AlgebraicSimplifierVisitor` hold over the reals but not under IEEE 754: if `exp(A)` overflows to `inf`, eager evaluation gives `log(inf) = inf`, but the unconditional fold silently returns the finite `A` instead, changing both the value and the gradient. Reproduces identically under JAX and TF-XLA, confirming it's an XLA-level bug rather than either framework's own code.

This file already gates this class of transform behind fast-math elsewhere (e.g. `A - A => 0` is unsound for `NaN` and sits behind `enable_fast_math()`), so both folds now use that same guard rather than a new mechanism.

The originating report also mentions `exp(log(x))` losing precision under GPU flush-to-zero — couldn't find that fold in `HandleExp`, so it's out of scope here.

Fixes #42732

Copybara import of the project:

--
2280c15950d4756b11f7029a1be76c987fbfaee1 by Sumukh Chaluvaraju <sumukhchaluvaraj@gmail.com>:

fix(algebraic_simplifier): gate unsound inverse-function folds behind fast-math

Two identities in the algebraic simplifier hold over the reals but not
under IEEE 754 float semantics, and were applied unconditionally:

  ln(exp(A)) => A       (HandleLog)
  sqrt(A*A) => |A|      (HandleSqrt)

If exp(A) overflows to inf (A >= ~88.7 for f32, ~709.8 for f64), eager
evaluation gives log(inf) = inf, but the folded form silently returns
the finite A instead -- changing both the forward value and the
gradient (unfolded: chain rule through an overflowing exp gives NaN;
folded: the identity gives a gradient of 1). The same divergence
applies to sqrt(A*A) for |A| > ~1.84e19 in f32. Verified directly
against current main: both HandleLog and HandleSqrt apply their
respective fold with no check on the input range at all.

This is exactly the class of transformation this file already
restricts to fast-math mode elsewhere -- e.g. "A - A => 0" a few
functions above HandleLog, which is unsound when A is NaN (NaN - NaN is
NaN, not 0). Applied the same options_.enable_fast_math() guard to
both folds, following that precedent rather than introducing a new
mechanism.

The pattern surfaces in manual (unstabilized) log-sum-exp and Euclidean
norm computations, where the whole point of computing log(exp(x)) or
sqrt(x^2) is often to detect the overflow, not to have it silently
elided by the compiler. Confirmed cross-framework: the same 89.0 ->
inf (eager) vs 89.0 (jit) divergence reproduces under both TensorFlow
XLA and JAX, consistent with the root cause being XLA's HLO-level
simplifier rather than either framework's own code.

Also updates three existing tests (LnExp, LnExpDiv,
SquaredComplexSqrtIsFloat) that exercised these folds under
default_options_, to explicitly opt into fast-math, since the
transformation they test is now gated. Adds three new regression
tests confirming the folds do NOT fire under default options
(preserving IEEE 754 overflow semantics) and still fire correctly
under explicit fast-math.

A third pattern mentioned in the originating report -- exp(log(x)) => x
losing precision under GPU flush-to-zero for subnormal x -- was not
found in HandleExp as currently written (it only implements exp(0) =>
1), so it is out of scope for this change; it may live in a different
pass or may not reproduce against current main.

Fixes #42732

--

title: PR #45822: [XLA:GPU] Add RHS transpose fold test for GEMM rewriter
body:
PR #45822: [XLA:GPU] Add RHS transpose fold test for GEMM rewriter

Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45822

📝 Summary of Changes
Added `RhsArgTransposeFoldCheck` to `gemm_rewriter_test.cc`.
The test checks that `GemmRewriter` folds a transpose on the RHS operand into the cuBLASLt matmul custom-call, updating the RHS contracting dimension from 0 on the transposed shape `f32[3,4]` to 1 on the original shape `f32[4,3]`.

🎯 Justification
`GemmRewriter` has coverage for folding a transpose on the LHS operand via `ArgTransposeFoldCheck`, but the corresponding positive RHS case was not covered.
This adds symmetric coverage for RHS transpose folding and helps protect the dot-to-GEMM rewrite path around transpose removal and contracting dimension updates.

🚀 Kind of Contribution
🧪 Tests

📊 Benchmark
Not applicable. This is a test-only change.

🧪 Unit Tests
Added `RhsArgTransposeFoldCheck` in `gemm_rewriter_test.cc`.
Verified locally:
bazel test //xla/backends/gpu/transforms:gemm_rewriter_test \
  --config=clang_local --test_output=errors
Result: PASSED.

🧪 Execution Tests
The test uses `RunAndCompare` with a minimal 2D `f32` dot + RHS transpose HLO and verifies the optimized HLO with `MatchOptimizedHlo`.

body:
Add input validation in WriteScalarSummaryOp::Compute (summary_kernels.cc) to verify that the `value` tensor contains exactly one element before delegating to the summary writer.

For invalid inputs (such as empty or multi-element tensors), return `errors::InvalidArgument` instead of allowing the operation to dereference an invalid tensor, preventing a segmentation fault and surfacing a Python `tf.errors.InvalidArgumentError`.

This change preserves the existing behavior for valid inputs while preventing crashes caused by invalid `value` tensors.

Also add graph and eager mode regression tests in `summary_ops_test.py` covering valid inputs as well as empty and multi-element tensors.

Fixes #113060.

title: [keras] Fix AUC.result returning 0 for single-class ROC inputs
body:
When y_true contains only one class (all-positive or all-negative) ROC-AUC is undefined. AUC.result() returned 0.0 because div_no_nan silently maps 0/0 to 0 making all fp-rate differences zero in the Riemann sum.

Checks true_positives[0] and false_positives[0] at threshold -epsilon (where all examples are predicted positive) and returns NaN if either class is absent. 

Fixes #41081

title: Fix WatchGlobalProcessInfo infinite retry on service incarnation mismatch
body:
Fix WatchGlobalProcessInfo infinite retry on service incarnation mismatch

When the coordination service restarts (e.g., after task preemption
triggers a full pod reallocation), surviving tasks hold a stale
service incarnation ID. Their WatchTasksAsync RPCs fail with
"wrong service incarnation", and the WatchGlobalProcessInfo loop
retries indefinitely because it never checks the agent error state.

Check agent.IsError() after a failed WatchTasksAsync. If the agent
has entered an unrecoverable ERROR state (set by the WatchTasksAsync
callback on incarnation mismatch), return the error instead of
retrying. Transient errors that do not trigger SetError continue
to be retried.

Validation: tested on a 256-chip (64-task) multi-host JAX workload
where a single task hit a coordination shutdown barrier timeout
(DEADLINE_EXCEEDED, 63/64 tasks reached barrier). Without the fix,
all remaining tasks entered an infinite retry loop logging "wrong
service incarnation" and hung indefinitely, requiring manual kill.
With the fix, the agent.IsError() check detected the unrecoverable
ERROR state and returned immediately, allowing all tasks to exit
cleanly via std::quick_exit(42) with no manual intervention.

title: [XLA] Don't crash in Identical on constant HLO if literal dropped
body:
[XLA] Don't crash in Identical on constant HLO if literal dropped

Currently if one tries to call Identical on two HLO instructions and
both are constant instructions that end up in a literal comparison, the
comparator will crash if the underlying literal has been dropped. Fix
this by checking for the presence of a literal on both sides first.

We choose to make the comparator conservative and have it return false
if either of the two compared HLO instructions don't have a literal.
They'll already compare on pointer equality, so this is just being
conservative: if any literals were dropped, they may have previously
been equal, but we'll assume they were distinct.

While we're here, let's drop the cast to HloSliceInstruction, which
turns out to be harmless (literal is called on HloInstruction, which
just casts to HloConstantInstruction anyway) but is still confusing. My
guess is that it's a copy-paste error.

title: PR #121730: Fix crash in SparseApplyMomentum with a grad of mismatched rank
body:
PR #121730: Fix crash in SparseApplyMomentum with a grad of mismatched rank

Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/pull/121730

Fixes #94131.

### Problem

`tf.raw_ops.ResourceSparseApplyMomentum` (and `SparseApplyMomentum`) crashes the process when `grad` has fewer dimensions than `var`:

```python
import numpy as np, tensorflow as tf
var = tf.Variable(np.zeros((6, 6), np.float32))
accum = tf.Variable(np.zeros((6, 6), np.float32))
tf.raw_ops.ResourceSparseApplyMomentum(
    var=var.handle, accum=accum.handle,
    lr=0.1, grad=tf.zeros([10]), indices=[0, 1, 2], momentum=0.9)  # crash
```

`SparseApplyMomentumOp::Compute` validates the per-dimension sizes of `grad` against `var` with a loop over `var.dims()`:

```cpp
for (int d = 1; d < var.dims(); d++) {
  OP_REQUIRES(ctx, var.dim_size(d) == grad.dim_size(d), ...);
}
```

but it never checks that `grad` has the same rank as `var` first. When `grad` is lower-rank, `grad.dim_size(d)` reads past `grad`'s rank and aborts the process.

### Fix

Add the same `grad.dims() == var.dims()` guard that the sibling sparse-apply kernels already perform before this loop (e.g. `SparseApplyProximalGradientDescentOp` and `SparseApplyAdagradOp` in the same file). This makes the subsequent `grad.dim_size(d)` accesses well-defined and turns the crash into an `InvalidArgument` error.

Added a regression test (`testResourceSparseApplyMomentumInvalidGradRank`).
Copybara import of the project:

--
cf38b7c395bb562ae5e93dff78a2f16412e33ca0 by Sai Ramineni <csramineni@gmail.com>:

Validate grad rank in the sparse apply ops to prevent a crash

The sparse apply kernels run a per-dimension shape-checking loop over
var.dims() that reads grad.dim_size(d) without first checking grad has
the same rank as var; a lower-rank grad reads past grad's rank and
crashes the process.

Add the grad.dims() == var.dims() gate the Adagrad ops already use to the
remaining sparse apply ops (Adadelta, ProximalGradientDescent,
ProximalAdagrad, AdagradDA, Ftrl, Momentum, KerasMomentum, RMSProp,
CenteredRMSProp), with a regression test.

title: PR #45597: [XLA:GPU] Add SKIP_PROFILED command buffer update mode
body:
PR #45597: [XLA:GPU] Add SKIP_PROFILED command buffer update mode

Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45597

## 📝 Summary of Changes

This PR adds the `SKIP_PROFILED` command-buffer update mode. The mode
automatically includes command-buffer-referenced preallocated temp allocations
in the final VA-remap set while profiling other eligible non-constant
allocations during the first three successful executions. Stable profile
candidates are combined with the temp allocations so subsequent command-buffer
executions can treat the final set as persistent and avoid unnecessary address
updates.

- Adds `DebugOptions::SKIP_PROFILED` and routes it through the GPU executable
  buffer-allocation policy.
- Adds a per-executor profile state machine to
  `GpuExecutableVaRemapAllocator`: `kProfiling` transitions exactly once to
  `kActive` when an automatic temp or safe profile candidate exists, or to
  `kDisabled` when the final remap set is empty.
- Automatically selects preallocated temp buffers and profiles eligible
  parameter and live-out candidates while filtering unstable addresses,
  non-VMM-backed parameters, and incompatible shared parameter buffers.
- Counts only successful executions toward the profiling window and passes
  `std::nullopt` for persistent allocation indices while profiling, followed by
  one absent-to-present transition to the final persistent set.
- Uses the parameter-remapping support from #45595 and output-buffer remapping
  support from #45596. Parameters and outputs remain externally owned in the
  const owning allocation table; finalized external addresses are mapped into
  an execution-only allocation-table copy immediately before execution.
- Preserves the base allocator fallback when VMM remapping is unavailable or
  there are neither automatic temp buffers nor stable profile candidates.

This PR is stacked on #45596, **[XLA:GPU] Support VA remapping of output buffer
allocations**. It is part 3 of 3 and, together with #45595 and #45596,
supersedes the closed pre-split PR #45568.

## 🎯 Justification

`SKIP_TEMP` can stabilize preallocated temporary buffers, but parameters and
live-out buffers may also keep stable physical addresses across repeated
executions. Updating command-buffer node addresses for those stable allocations
adds avoidable runtime overhead.

`SKIP_PROFILED` discovers address stability for each executable/executor pair
instead of assuming every dynamic allocation is stable. Workloads with
repeatable allocation behavior can therefore stabilize more
command-buffer-visible addresses, while unstable or unsupported workloads retain
the existing fallback behavior. Mapping finalized owning addresses immediately
before execution also keeps donation and copy-protection changes compatible with
the const owning-allocation API introduced by the lower PRs.

## 🚀 Kind of Contribution

✨ New Feature

## 📊 Benchmark (for Performance Improvements)

No benchmark result is claimed in this draft. The PR adds the policy and
correctness coverage; a public-HLO performance benchmark can be added separately
when reporting workload-specific speedups.

## 🧪 Unit Tests:

- Extended `GpuExecutableTest.CommandBufferAllocationPolicy` expectations for
  automatic temp-buffer selection in `SKIP_PROFILED`.
- Added host-only fake-VMM coverage for profiling, transition, active remapping,
  and disabled fallback states.
- Verifies a temp-only executable transitions to an active persistent temp set,
  and an unstable parameter cannot disqualify an automatically selected temp.
- Verifies stable parameter and output selection, including non-granularity-sized
  externally owned outputs.
- Verifies failed executions do not advance the three-successful-execution
  profiling window.
- Verifies fallback for unstable and non-VMM-backed parameters and incompatible
  shared parameter buffers.
- Verifies copy protection both during profiling and after transition: the
  owning table retains the finalized external address while execution uses the
  stable reservation address.

## 🧪 Execution Tests:

- Added one-device `VmmTest` coverage using a two-GEMM command-buffer workload
  across profiling, transition, and remapped executions, checking numerical
  correctness on every run.
- Verifies one-time profile selection, automatic temp inclusion, VA-reservation
  creation and reuse, and successful deallocation of externally returned output
  buffers.
- Added a non-VMM allocator execution test verifying the workload remains
  correct and creates no VA reservation when remapping is unavailable.

title: PR #44653: Validate buffer slices during deserialization
body:
PR #44653: Validate buffer slices during deserialization

Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/44653

## Summary

- validate `BufferAllocation::Slice::FromProto` rejects negative offsets and sizes
- reject serialized slices whose `[offset, offset + size)` range exceeds the referenced allocation
- add regression coverage for malformed `BufferAllocationSliceProto` values

## Why

`BufferAllocation::Slice::FromProto` reconstructs runtime buffer slices from serialized executable data. It previously validated only `buffer_allocation_index`, while the compile-time assignment path guarantees that `offset >= 0`, `size >= 0`, and `offset + size <= allocation.size()`. This change enforces that same invariant before constructing the slice.

## Testing

- `git diff --check`
- `clang++ -std=c++17 -fsanitize=address -g -DNDEBUG -DAPPLY_FIX poc_primitive_asan.cc -o /tmp/openxla_poc_fixed && /tmp/openxla_poc_fixed` from the local reproduction bundle; the fixed logic rejects the malformed slice
- attempted `bazel test //xla/service:buffer_assignment_test`; local sparse-checkout setup initially missed top-level `third_party` and `tools`, and after adding them the invocation stayed in Bazel repository mapping until interrupted. Retrying with `--noexperimental_repo_remote_exec` fails during repository initialization because `rules_ml_toolchain` requires that experimental flag.

title: PR #45682: pjrt: propagate Prepare failure on non-two-phase multi-device Execute
body:
PR #45682: pjrt: propagate Prepare failure on non-two-phase multi-device Execute

Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45682

## The bug

`CommonPjRtLoadedExecutable::Execute`'s multi-device path calls `ExecutePrepareWithOomRetries` per device, then — only when `supports_two_phase_launch()` is true — checks the returned status inside the two-phase barrier block. `CpuClient` overrides `supports_two_phase_launch()` to `false`, so on a multi-device CpuClient a Prepare failure is never checked and execution falls through to `ExecuteLaunch(*launch_args, ...)` with `launch_args.executable` still null → null-deref crash instead of a clean error.

## The fix

Add an `else if (!launch_status.ok())` branch after the two-phase block: record the failure in `results[i]`, decrement `launching` under `mu`, return. Same shape as the two-phase failure path minus the barrier it doesn't participate in.

## Scope

`CpuClient` is the only in-tree client with `supports_two_phase_launch()==false`. Two-phase clients (GPU/TPU stream-executor) are unchanged — the diff is purely additive.

## Test

`MultiDevicePrepareFailurePropagatesError` in `[cpu_client_test.cc](http://cpu_client_test.cc/)`: 2-device CpuClient, 2-replica identity program, wrong-size buffer on device 1 so `CheckBufferCompatibilities` rejects it during Prepare. Asserts `Execute()` returns an error (not a crash) and that the error comes from the per-device Prepare path.

launch_args.executable still null and crashed.

title: PR #45684: pjrt_c_api_gpu: plumb max_inflight_computations through PJRT C-API create options
body:
PR #45684: pjrt_c_api_gpu: plumb max_inflight_computations through PJRT C-API create options

Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45684

b261c3b677 ("Respect `GpuOptions::max_inflight_computations` in SE GPU") added `GpuClientOptions::max_inflight_computations` and wired it through the StreamExecutor GPU client, but did not add the corresponding key to the GPU PJRT C API plugin's create-options allowlist. Since `ValidateCreateOptions` rejects unrecognized keys, passing `"max_inflight_computations"` via the plugin path (e.g. JAX's `jax_pjrt_client_create_options`) fails client creation with `Unexpected option name passed to PJRT_Client_Create`, so the field can't be set from Python.

This PR adds the key to `kExpectedOptionNameAndTypes` and forwards it to `GpuClientOptions`. The value is validated to be in `(0, INT32_MAX]`: the field is `int`, and a zero value would construct a compute semaphore that blocks forever on the first `Execute()`. When the option is omitted, the `GpuClientOptions` default (32) is preserved; disabling the semaphore entirely (`std::nullopt`) remains C++-only, since expressing "no limit" through an int64 option would need a sentinel value.

This is GPU-specific because `"max_inflight_computations"` is already among the TPU plugin's published create-option names (`pjrt::tpu_configs::kMaxInflightComputations`, `xla/pjrt/c/pjrt_c_api_tpu_constants.h`); this change brings the GPU plugin to parity.

Tested: `pjrt_c_api_gpu_test` — `PjrtCApiGpuMaxInflightComputationsTest.{Valid,Invalid}OptionsParsing`.

plugin options (e.g. jax_plugins.cuda.initialize()). The value is validated

than silently producing a zero-capacity compute semaphore that deadlocks at

the PJRT C API layer doesn't yet accept it from the create-options dict, so
there's no way to set it from Python. This closes that gap.

title: Check all positions of operand values in BufferAssigner
body:
Check all positions of operand values in BufferAssigner

When an HloValue has multiple positions in the module (such as when passed into
a while loop tuple or bitcast view), the live range's end_position might point
to one of these secondary positions rather than the instruction position that the
downstream consumer directly consumes. Checking all positions allows the consumer
to share the buffer in-place when their live ranges abut.

title: PR #45871: [HLO] Validate operand count and shape in recv/conditional/rng-bit-generator/reduce-window parsing
body:
PR #45871: [HLO] Validate operand count and shape in recv/conditional/rng-bit-generator/reduce-window parsing

Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45871

### Problem
Four opcode handlers in `HloParserImpl::CreateInstruction` read a parsed operand or the result shape without validating it, so malformed-but-lexable HLO aborts the parser (a `CHECK` failure or an out-of-bounds read) instead of returning an error `Status`:

- **`recv`** — `CreateRecv(shape->tuple_shapes(0), ...)` calls `tuple_shapes(0)` without checking the result shape is a non-empty tuple, so a non-tuple shape `CHECK`-fails in `Shape::tuple_state()`:
  ```
  tok = token[] after-all()
  ROOT recv = f32[10] recv(tok)
  ```
- **`conditional`** — reads `operands[0]->shape()` immediately after a count-agnostic `ParseOperands`, so zero operands reads past the end (the later count check is downstream):
  ```
  ROOT c = f32[] conditional()
  ```
- **`rng-bit-generator`** — reads `operands[0]` after a count-agnostic `ParseOperands`:
  ```
  ROOT r = (u64[2], u32[4]) rng-bit-generator(), algorithm=rng_three_fry
  ```
- **`reduce-window`** — its operand-count check only rejects an *odd* count, so zero operands passes and the shape-inference lambda reads `operands[0]`/`operands[1]`:
  ```
  ROOT rw = reduce-window(), to_apply=add
  ```

### Fix
- `recv`: guard `!shape->IsTuple() || shape->tuple_shapes().empty()` before `tuple_shapes(0)`, mirroring the existing `kInfeed` guard.
- `conditional`, `reduce-window`: return a parse error when `operands` is empty.
- `rng-bit-generator`: pass `ParseOperands(..., /*expected_size=*/1)`.

### Tests
Four `HloParserTest` cases assert these malformed inputs now return an error instead of crashing.

---
Disclosure: this contribution was authored with an AI coding assistant (Claude) and reviewed before submission.

title: PR #45877: [XLA:GPU][oneAPI/SPIR-V] Skip compilation and loading of empty constants modules
body: PR #45877: [XLA:GPU][oneAPI/SPIR-V] Skip compilation and loading of empty constants modules Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45877 Hello, xla and intel team, in my journey to understand and improve the SPIR-V compilation pipeline in PJRT, I found that XLA always passes the constants LLVM module to the target compiler, even when the module is empty. I observed 2 time the `oneAPI::zeModuleCreate_SPIRVToNative` function being called, once for the kernel and once for the empty constants module. This can be seen by a [perfetto](https://perfetto.dev/) screenshot. I think the empty constants module can be skipped entirely, avoiding unnecessary compilation and module loading. ## 📝 Summary of Changes * Skip target-binary compilation when the emitted constants LLVM module contains no functions or globals. * Skip device-module loading whenever the constants binary is empty, instead of handling this case only for CUDA. * Verify that constants metadata is not present without a corresponding constants binary. * Add a regression test covering resolution of an empty constants binary. ## 🎯 Justification Previously, XLA always passed the constants LLVM module to the target compiler, even when the module was empty. \ At runtime, loading an empty constants module was skipped only for CUDA. This unnecessarily requires other GPU backends to support compilation and loading of empty modules. \ In particular, it blocks SPIR-V-oriented GPU compilation paths for HLO modules that do not emit device constants. The change makes the empty-constants case platform-independent. Constant-free GPU workloads benefit from avoiding an invalid or unnecessary constants compilation and module-loading step. ## 🚀 Kind of Contribution 🐛 Bug Fix, 🧪 Tests ## 📊 Benchmark This is mainly a correctness and backend-portability fix, but the purpose is to avoid unnecessary compilation and module loading for empty constants modules so it may improve performance in such cases. ## 🧪 Unit Tests Added `GpuModuleGlobalsTest.EmptyBinarySkipsModuleLoading` to `gpu_executable_test.cc`. The test uses mock stream and stream-executor objects to verify that resolving an empty constants binary: * does not query the platform; * does not call `LoadModule`; and * returns an empty globals map. Test target: ```bash ONEAPI_DEVICE_SELECTOR=level_zero:0 bazel test //xla/service/gpu:gpu_executable_test \ --test_filter=GpuModuleGlobalsTest.EmptyBinarySkipsModuleLoading ``` ```bash bazel build //xla/pjrt/c:pjrt_c_api_gpu_plugin.so \ --config=sycl_hermetic \ --repo_env=ONEAPI_VERSION=2026.0 \ --repo_env=OS=ubuntu_24.10 ``` ## 🧪 Execution Tests None added. The changed behavior concerns empty constants-module handling and is covered by the focused hardware-independent unit test above. In this case, no target binary is produced. Update GpuModuleGlobals to skip module loading whenever the constants binary is empty, independent of the GPU platform. This prevents backends such as oneAPI from attempting to load an empty module specification. This avoids unnecessary backend compilation and first-execution module creation for programs without device constants. -- 1fdbf05e74e1154ae99617466126688ddee5395f by Kevin Barre : [GPU] Validate constants metadata presence when binary is empty in Resolve function -- e2c7c1091f9b4a9f23423c9df7f1ab6a124212db by Kevin Barre : [GPU] Improve readability by introducing a boolean for constants module check -- b7446a91a6c1664f50d1cb683abc6d26ee9014fc by Kevin Barre : [GPU] Add mock stream and executor to GpuModuleGlobals test for empty binary handling -- eaf8cb7981174e4fb2a0c32b7c18c52201cf8bec by Kevin Barre : [LLVM] Introduce FileCheckInput function to handle empty IR strings in verification Merging this change closes #45877 FUTURE_COPYBARA_INTEGRATE_REVIEW=https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45877 from zml:kevin/upstream/compilation-spirv eaf8cb7981174e4fb2a0c32b7c18c52201cf8bec

PR #45596: [XLA:GPU] Support VA remapping of output buffer allocations

Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45596

## 📝 Summary of Changes

This PR extends command-buffer virtual-address remapping to allocations that
escape to the caller (`maybe_live_out`) while preserving the const ownership
model introduced by #45595.

- Keeps selected live-out allocations at normal allocator-owned addresses in
  the owning `BufferAllocations` table.
- Extends `GetRemappedBufferAllocations` to map finalized selected input/output
  addresses into their stable reservation slices immediately before execution.
- Rewrites only the non-owning execution copy, so result handling and teardown
  continue to see external, deallocatable addresses.
- Handles donated and copy-protected aliases by mapping after output setup has
  finalized the owning address table.
- Extends the host-only fake-VMM tests to cover stable execution aliases,
  logical external sizes, output deallocation, and deferred alias cleanup.

This PR is stacked on #45595, **[XLA:GPU] Support VA remapping of parameter
buffer allocations**. It is part 2 of 3 splitting the former #45568
implementation; #45597 adds the `SKIP_PROFILED` mode.

## 🎯 Justification

Live-out buffers can outlive an execution scope, so the caller must retain a
normal allocator-owned address that remains valid and can be passed to
`Deallocate()`. At the same time, recorded command buffers need a stable
reservation address.

Building a separate execution allocation table provides both properties
without mutating the owning table or removing its const qualifier. Deferring
the mapping until execution also ensures donation and copy protection have
already selected the final external address.

## 🚀 Kind of Contribution

✨ New Feature

## 📊 Benchmark (for Performance Improvements)

Not applicable as a standalone change. This PR adds output-buffer remapping
support but does not independently enable a new command-buffer update mode.
Performance evaluation belongs to the final `SKIP_PROFILED` policy in #45597.

## 🧪 Unit Tests:

- Extends the host-only fake-VMM allocator test with a non-granularity-aligned
  remapped live-out allocation.
- Verifies the owning table contains the logical-size external address before
  and after execution.
- Verifies execution receives the same stable reservation address across runs
  and maps the correct raw physical allocation.
- Verifies the external address is deallocatable and all aliases are released.

## 🧪 Execution Tests:

No standalone execution test is added for this enabling layer. End-to-end
live-out selection, execution, and output deallocation are covered by the
stacked `SKIP_PROFILED` change in #45597.

title: Fix NaN gradient of tf.math.igamma at a=1, x=0
body:
Fixes #123578

 _IgammaGrad computed partial_x = exp(-x + (a-1)*log(x) - lgamma(a)).
  
At a==1, x==0 the term (a-1)*log(x) is 0 * -inf = NaN, so the gradient of tf.math.igamma w.r.t. x is NaN there. The true derivative is d/dx igamma(1, x) = e^-x = 1. Use xlogy(a-1, x) instead, matching _BetaincGrad which already does this. Other regimes unchanged (a>1 -> 0, a<1 -> +inf, nonzero x identical). Adds IgammaGradTest.

Reproduced on tf-nightly 2.22.0-dev20260718.

title: tf.math.igamma gradient w.r.t. x returns NaN at a=1, x=0 (expected 1.0)

labels.name: comp:core
labels.description: issues related to core part of tensorflow

body:
### Issue type

Bug

### Have you reproduced the bug with TensorFlow Nightly?

Yes

### Source

binary

### TensorFlow version

tf-nightly 2.22.0-dev20260718 (git: v1.12.1-142552-g4f1b7de8a37)

### Custom code

Yes

### OS platform and distribution

Linux Ubuntu 24.04.4 LTS

### Mobile device

_No response_

### Python version

3.12.3

### Bazel version

_No response_

### GCC/compiler version

_No response_

### CUDA/cuDNN version

_No response_

### GPU model and memory

_No response_

### Current behavior?

The gradient of tf.math.igamma(a, x) with respect to x is NaN at exactly
  a = 1, x = 0.

  igamma(1, x) = 1 - e^-x, so d/dx igamma(1, x) = e^-x, which equals 1 at
  x = 0. The value is finite and well-defined, so the correct gradient is
  1.0 — not NaN.

  Root cause: _IgammaGrad in tensorflow/python/ops/math_grad.py computes
  partial_x = exp(-x + (a - 1) * log(x) - lgamma(a)). At a = 1, x = 0 the
  term (a - 1) * log(x) evaluates to 0 * -inf = NaN. The neighboring
  _BetaincGrad already avoids this by using xlogy for the analogous term.

  Other regimes are correct: a > 1 gives 0, and a < 1 gives +inf (a genuine
  singularity). Only the a = 1, x = 0 boundary produces the spurious NaN.


### Standalone code to reproduce the issue

```shell
import tensorflow as tf

  a = tf.constant(1.0)
  x = tf.constant(0.0)
  with tf.GradientTape() as tape:
      tape.watch(x)
      y = tf.math.igamma(a, x)   # igamma(1, x) = 1 - e^-x

  print(tape.gradient(y, x))     # tf.Tensor(nan, ...) -- expected 1.0
```

### Relevant log output

```shell
tf.Tensor(nan, shape=(), dtype=float32)
```

title: Fix bfloat16 CPU Rsqrt length-dependent double rounding

labels.name: comp:core
labels.description: issues related to core part of tensorflow

body:
Fixes #123551

### Description

On CPU, `tf.math.rsqrt` with `bfloat16` returned different values for the same input depending on tensor length. For `0.53125`:

- lengths 1–7 (scalar Eigen path): `1.3671875`
- lengths ≥ 8 (AVX packet path): `1.375`
- XLA / correctly rounded reference: `1.375`

### Root cause

Eigen's default scalar `rsqrt` is `1 / sqrt(x)`. For bfloat16 both operations cast back to bfloat16, so the scalar path double-rounds. The packet path converts the packet to float, computes float `rsqrt`, and casts once — so results change at the SIMD packet boundary.

### Solution

Specialize `Eigen::internal::scalar_rsqrt_op<bfloat16>` in `cwise_ops.h` to compute `rsqrt` in float and cast to bfloat16 once.
Keep `packetOp` / `PacketAccess` so CPU vectorization is unchanged.

### Changes

- `tensorflow/core/kernels/cwise_ops.h` — bfloat16 `scalar_rsqrt_op`  specialization
- `tensorflow/python/kernel_tests/math_ops/cwise_ops_unary_test.py` —  compare bf16 rsqrt against float32 reference; add
  `testBFloat16RsqrtLengthIndependent` for lengths `1, 7, 8, 9, 16, 32`

### Tests

- `//tensorflow/python/kernel_tests/math_ops:cwise_ops_unary_test`

title: reject zero-element non-memcpyable components in UncompressElement

labels.name: comp:core
labels.description: issues related to core part of tensorflow

body: UncompressElement sizes and fills the nonmemcpyable scratch buffer only for components with a non-zero element count, but the third pass walks it for every non-memcpyable non-string component regardless of shape. A CompressedElement carrying a DT_VARIANT component of shape [0] with a large uncompressed_bytes reserves no space and adds no iovec, so the size reconciliation against the snappy length still passes and ParseFromString then reads an attacker-chosen length past a zero-length heap buffer; this decodes straight from a Variant, so tf.data snapshot files and tf.raw_ops.UncompressElement reach it. Reject zero-element components there so the third pass walks exactly the bytes the first pass reserved.

title: [XLA] Overwrite frontend attributes during call inlining for nested fusion scopes.
body:
[XLA] Overwrite frontend attributes during call inlining for nested fusion scopes.

When inlining calls, use assignment to overwrite existing frontend attributes like MUST_FUSE IDs. This ensures that in nested must-fuse regions, the outermost scope's ID is applied to all instructions within the inlined call, unifying the entire region under a single logical ID. This prevents fragmentation of the intended fusion scope.

title: CPU bfloat16 tf.math.rsqrt output depends on tensor length (scalar vs vectorized path)

labels.name: comp:core
labels.description: issues related to core part of tensorflow

body:
### Issue type

Bug

### Have you reproduced the bug with TensorFlow Nightly?

Yes

### Source

binary

### TensorFlow version

`tf-nightly 2.22.0-dev20260717` (`v1.12.1-142552-g4f1b7de8a37`)

### Custom code

Yes

### OS platform and distribution

Ubuntu 20.04.6 LTS under WSL2 (x86_64). The same behavior also reproduces with TensorFlow 2.20.0 on Ubuntu 24.04 x86_64.

### Mobile device

_No response_

### Python version

3.11.7

### Bazel version

_No response_

### GCC/compiler version

_No response_

### CUDA/cuDNN version

CPU-only reproducer

### GPU model and memory

CPU-only reproducer

### Current behavior?

On CPU, the bfloat16 result of the elementwise `tf.math.rsqrt` operation depends on the total number of elements in the input tensor.

For the same input value `0.53125`, tensors with 1 through 7 elements return `1.3671875`, while the tested lengths 8 through 32 return `1.375`. A non-XLA `tf.function` follows the same length-dependent behavior. XLA returns `1.375` for both representative lengths.

The high-precision value is approximately `1.37198868`, which rounds to `1.375` in bfloat16. Therefore the length-8 CPU path and XLA match the rounded reference, while the length-1 CPU path is one bfloat16 ULP lower.

This also reproduces with `TF_ENABLE_ONEDNN_OPTS=0`, so it is not caused by oneDNN. In a sweep of all 385 bfloat16 inputs in `[0.5, 4.0]`, the length-1 eager path differed from the rounded float64 reference at 97 inputs; the length-8 path and XLA matched the reference at those inputs.

The CPU kernel registers bfloat16 `Rsqrt` through `functor::rsqrt`, which delegates to Eigen's `scalar_rsqrt_op`. The observed length threshold suggests that Eigen's scalar and packet paths use different intermediate precision. For the example above, the scalar result is also equal to `tf.math.reciprocal(tf.math.sqrt(x))` evaluated in bfloat16, suggesting a double-rounding path. This root-cause explanation is an inference from the outputs and current source structure.

I expected an elementwise operation applied to the same value and dtype to produce the same result regardless of unrelated tensor length.

Related issues:

- #86607 reported bfloat16 `Rsqrt` differences between CPU and GPU and was closed after being treated as expected cross-backend numerical variation. This report is different because it compares the same CPU kernel and changes only an unrelated tensor length; it also reproduces on the current nightly.
- #123104 reports a different bfloat16 CPU scalar-functor problem for `tf.sigmoid`; this report concerns `Rsqrt` and a separate scalar/packet implementation.

### Standalone code to reproduce the issue

```python
import os

os.environ["CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES"] = ""
os.environ["TF_ENABLE_ONEDNN_OPTS"] = "0"

import tensorflow as tf


def rsqrt(x):
    return tf.math.rsqrt(x)


value = 0.53125
for length in (1, 7, 8):
    x = tf.constant([value] * length, dtype=tf.bfloat16)
    print(f"eager length={length}:", float(tf.cast(rsqrt(x)[0], tf.float32)))

x = tf.constant([value], dtype=tf.bfloat16)
graph_rsqrt = tf.function(rsqrt)
xla_rsqrt = tf.function(rsqrt, jit_compile=True)
reference = tf.cast(tf.math.rsqrt(tf.cast(x, tf.float64)), tf.bfloat16)

print("TensorFlow:", tf.__version__)
print("Git version:", tf.version.GIT_VERSION)
print("graph length=1:", float(tf.cast(graph_rsqrt(x)[0], tf.float32)))
print("XLA length=1:  ", float(tf.cast(xla_rsqrt(x)[0], tf.float32)))
print("rounded reference:", float(tf.cast(reference[0], tf.float32)))
```

### Relevant log output

```text
eager length=1: 1.3671875
eager length=7: 1.3671875
eager length=8: 1.375
TensorFlow: 2.22.0-dev20260717
Git version: v1.12.1-142552-g4f1b7de8a37
graph length=1: 1.3671875
XLA length=1:   1.375
rounded reference: 1.375
```

TensorFlow's CPU `Rsqrt` kernel registration:
https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/master/tensorflow/core/kernels/cwise_op_rsqrt.cc

The unary functor definition:
https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/master/tensorflow/core/kernels/cwise_ops.h

title: Avoid grabbing the `TileAssignment` mutex in the common case.
body:
Avoid grabbing the `TileAssignment` mutex in the common case.

Before this change, many trivial accessor fuctions in `TileAssignment`
would acquire the mutex protecting the materialized array. This is
especially unfortunate for accessors like `num_dimensions()`, which can
be called in loops and which should always be able to obtain the value
cheaply (without acquiring any locks) from either `iota_` or the
materialized array.

This change makes `array_` an atomic pointer to enable it to be accessed
without locking. Accessors that can use either `iota_` or `array_` are
changed to try `iota_` by default unless obtaining the value from
`array_` is cheaper. After this change, the mutex is only acquired when
we materialize the array or access `shared_array_`.

title: Fix host memory leak in tf.data.Dataset.from_generator during eager execution
body:
## Bug fix: host RAM leak in `tf.data.Dataset.from_generator` (#123269)

**Reproduction:** Running `from_generator` in a loop leaks ~128 MB of host RSS over 500 iterations even after `del dataset` and `gc.collect()`. GPU VRAM is unaffected.

## Root cause

Every call to `from_generator` traces three closures via `StructuredFunctionWrapper` (`init_func`, `next_func`, `finalize_func`). During tracing those closures call `numpy_function` / `eager_py_func`, which routes through `_internal_py_func` in `script_ops.py`. There, a **strong reference** to each closure is stored:

```python
# script_ops.py – the leak
graph = ops.get_default_graph()
while True:          # walk up to outermost non-FuncGraph
    ...
graph._py_funcs_used_in_graph.append(func)   # ← accumulates forever
```

In eager mode the outermost non-FuncGraph is the **process-lifetime singleton** returned by `ops.get_default_graph()`. It is never destroyed, so `_py_funcs_used_in_graph` grows indefinitely. After 500 iterations, ~1 500 closures accumulate there (each capturing a `generator_state` object), accounting for the ~128 MB RSS growth.

## Fix

When `_internal_py_func` is called while already executing inside a `FuncGraph` (as happens during `StructuredFunctionWrapper` tracing), skip the walk-to-outermost-graph and keep the strong reference on the **FuncGraph itself**:

```python
if not isinstance(graph, func_graph.FuncGraph):
    while True:   # only walk up when NOT already in a FuncGraph
        ...
graph._py_funcs_used_in_graph.append(func)
```

The `FuncGraph`'s lifetime is tied to its `ConcreteFunction` → `StructuredFunctionWrapper` → `Dataset`. When `del dataset` runs and GC collects the chain, the `FuncGraph` is dismantled, `_py_funcs_used_in_graph` on it is released, and all three closures (and their `generator_state` captures) are freed.

In graph mode and in eager mode outside any `FuncGraph` the original behaviour is preserved unchanged.

Fixes #123269

title: Fix tf.raw_ops.RFFT crashing on fft_length=[0] instead of raising InvalidArgumentError
body:
## Bug fix: `tf.raw_ops.RFFT` with `fft_length=[0]` crashes the process

Fixes #123399

**Reproduction:**
```python
x = tf.fill([3, 3, 31], tf.constant(-1.0, dtype=tf.float32))
y = tf.raw_ops.RFFT(input=x, fft_length=tf.constant([0], dtype=tf.int32))
tf.shape(y).numpy()
# Aborted (core dumped) — DUCC FFT assertion: "axis length mismatch"
```

## Root cause

The input validation in `fft_ops.cc` checked `fft_length[i] >= 0`, allowing `fft_length=0` to pass through. With an FFT size of 0, the output shape is set to `[3, 3, 0]` and the call is forwarded to the DUCC FFT library, which fatally aborts with a C-level assertion instead of raising a recoverable Python exception.

## Fix

Change the guard in `fft_ops.cc` from `>= 0` to `> 0` and update the error message accordingly:

```cpp
// Before
OP_REQUIRES(ctx, fft_length_as_vec(i) >= 0,
            errors::InvalidArgument("fft_length[", i, "] must >= 0 ..."));

// After
OP_REQUIRES(ctx, fft_length_as_vec(i) > 0,
            errors::InvalidArgument("fft_length[", i, "] must be > 0 ..."));
```

After the fix, calling with `fft_length=[0]` raises a clean `InvalidArgumentError` before reaching the FFT kernel.

## Test

Added `test_zero_fft_length_raises` to `fft_ops_test.py` targeting `gen_spectral_ops.r_f_f_t` directly (the high-level `tf.signal.rfft` wrapper applies its own constraints and does not exercise this validation path).

title: PR #45263: Allow dynamic-update-slice into an unbounded-dynamic operand dimension
body:
PR #45263: Allow dynamic-update-slice into an unbounded-dynamic operand dimension

Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45263

### Problem
`InferDynamicUpdateSliceShape` rejects a valid `dynamic-update-slice` into an **unbounded-dynamic** operand dimension.

### Root cause
```cpp
const int64_t input_dim_size = operand_shape.dimensions(dim);
...
if (update_dim_size > input_dim_size) {   // input_dim_size == kUnboundedSize (INT64_MIN) for an unbounded dim
  return InvalidArgument("Update dim size %d greater than dynamic slice dimension: %d.", ...);
}
```
For an unbounded-dynamic operand dimension, `operand_shape.dimensions(dim)` is `kUnboundedSize` (`INT64_MIN`). Any concrete static update size (e.g. `5`) then satisfies `5 > INT64_MIN` and the op is rejected with a nonsensical bound (`-9223372036854775808`). The sibling `InferDynamicSliceShape` guards the identical comparison with `!IsUnboundedDynamicSize(input_dim_size)`, and this function already handles unbounded *update* sizes just above — so unbounded operands are in scope, but the operand side is unguarded.

### Fix
Guard with `!IsUnboundedDynamicSize(input_dim_size)`, matching `InferDynamicSliceShape`.

### Verification
Static reasoning: e.g. `operand = f32[?, 10]`, `update = f32[3, 5]` is rejected today (`3 > INT64_MIN`) and accepted with the fix; operand-static-with-too-large-update still errors; both-unbounded and operand-static-with-unbounded-update are unchanged. (No local Bazel build set up — happy to add a shape-inference test.)

Copybara import of the project:

--
1b4c86365393d200d45a372af006ea0a64b94a74 by Eylon Krause <eylon1909@gmail.com>:

Allow dynamic-update-slice into an unbounded-dynamic operand dimension

InferDynamicUpdateSliceShape rejected a static update dimension larger than
the operand dimension, but for an unbounded-dynamic operand dimension
operand_shape.dimensions(dim) is kUnboundedSize (INT64_MIN), so any concrete
update size trivially exceeds it and a valid op is rejected with a
nonsensical bound. Guard the comparison with
!IsUnboundedDynamicSize(input_dim_size), matching the sibling
InferDynamicSliceShape.

Merging this change closes #45263

title: SIGFPE in SequenceExample parsing with FixedLenSequenceFeature shape=[0]

labels.name: comp:core
labels.description: issues related to core part of tensorflow

body:
### Issue type

Bug

### Have you reproduced the bug with TensorFlow Nightly?

Yes

### Source

source

### TensorFlow version

tf.version.VERSION: 2.21.0-dev0+selfbuilt

### Custom code

Yes

### OS platform and distribution

Linux Ubuntu 22.04

### Mobile device

_No response_

### Python version

Python 3.10.12

### Bazel version

bazel-9.1.0

### GCC/compiler version

gcc (Ubuntu 11.4.0-1ubuntu1~22.04.2) 11.4.0

### CUDA/cuDNN version

_No response_

### GPU model and memory

_No response_

### Current behavior?

Calling TensorFlow SequenceExample parsing APIs with a FixedLenSequenceFeature whose shape contains a zero dimension can trigger a SIGFPE in TensorFlow's native fast parsing path.

I observed the same underlying crash through three public TensorFlow APIs:

tf.io.FixedLenSequenceFeature
tf.io.parse_sequence_example
tf.io.parse_single_sequence_example

These appear to be different entry points into the same root cause. In all cases, the crash points to TensorFlow's native ParseSequenceDenseFeatures implementation:

example_proto_fast_parsing.cc
tensorflow::example::(anonymous namespace)::ParseSequenceDenseFeatures(...)

The minimal reproducer below uses tf.io.parse_single_sequence_example with an empty SequenceExample and a single sequence feature spec:

sequence_features = {
    "k": tf.io.FixedLenSequenceFeature(
        shape=[0],
        dtype=tf.float32,
        allow_missing=True,
    )
}

In my ASan-enabled TensorFlow build, this does not fail with a clean, catchable TensorFlow exception. Instead, the process aborts with an AddressSanitizer FPE report in ParseSequenceDenseFeatures.

This can cause a denial-of-service condition when SequenceExample feature specifications are derived from malformed or untrusted input. A zero dimension in FixedLenSequenceFeature.shape should be rejected deterministically before entering the native fast parser, preferably with a recoverable Python exception such as InvalidArgumentError.

### Standalone code to reproduce the issue

```shell
import tensorflow as tf

serialized = tf.train.SequenceExample().SerializeToString()

sequence_features = {
    "k": tf.io.FixedLenSequenceFeature(
        shape=[0],
        dtype=tf.float32,
        allow_missing=True,
    )
}

tf.io.parse_single_sequence_example(
    serialized=serialized,
    sequence_features=sequence_features,
)
```

title: PR #45816: [XLA:GPU] Don't let rank-0 dimension-size scalars inherit op sharding
body:
PR #45816: [XLA:GPU] Don't let rank-0 dimension-size scalars inherit op sharding

Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45816

📝 Summary of Changes
Stop rank-0 dimension-size scalars from inheriting the enclosing op's sharding.

🎯 Justification
Shardy-partitioned MoE models (MaxText Mixtral, DeepSeek2, GPT-OSS) fail to compile. Their bf16 top-k routing lowers into an op that, under a tiled sharding, passes a multi-dimensional sharding onto a scalar, which is invalid and aborts compilation. This has been latent and only surfaced after this #44538

🚀 Kind of Contribution
🐛 Bug Fix

🧪 Unit Tests:
New case in `mhlo_to_hlo/tests/sharding.mlir`
Copybara import of the project:

--
e12c6475bdd83bfa3871338257b0308f925065a4 by Aleksei Nurmukhametov <anurmukh@amd.com>:

[XLA:GPU] Don't let rank-0 dimension-size scalars inherit op sharding

RemoveDynamicDimension/GetDimensionSize create a rank-0 constant to hold the
static size. The mhlo->hlo op writer applies each op's mhlo.sharding to
everything created while lowering that op, so under a rank>0 tiled sharding
the scalar picks it up and HloSharding::Validate fails. Clear the
sharding around the constant as other builder helpers do.

Merging this change closes #45816

title: Fix heap out-of-bounds write in CTCLoss when U=1.
body:
Fix heap out-of-bounds write in CTCLoss when U=1.

The `CTCLossCalculator` initialization logic assumed `U >= 2`, leading to invalid memory access in `CalculateForwardVariables` and `CalculateBackwardVariables` when the target sequence collapses to an empty sequence (`U=1`).

- In `CalculateForwardVariables`, unconditional access to `log_alpha->coeffRef(1, 0)` is now guarded with `if (U > 1)`.
- In `CalculateBackwardVariables`, the lower bound of the initialization loop is now clamped using `std::max(0, U - 2)` to prevent negative index access.
